title: Input & Output — Data Pipelines
key_insight: Input/output is not just about devices — it's about data transformation at every level of a system.
body:
## Input/Output as Data Flow

In software, I/O refers to any transfer of data between components — not just keyboards and screens.

### Types of I/O in a Software System

```
User types → Browser → HTTP request → Server → Database → Response → Screen
   (input)                (I/O)       (I/O)    (I/O)       (output)
```

Every arrow is I/O. Data moves between components constantly.

### I/O in an AI Pipeline

```
Raw text file  →  Tokenizer  →  Token IDs  →  Model  →  Logits  →  Softmax  →  Probabilities
  (disk I/O)       (transform)   (memory)    (compute)  (memory)  (compute)    (output)
```

Each stage has inputs and outputs. The output of one stage is the input of the next.

### Standard Streams

In Unix systems (Linux, Mac), every process has three I/O channels:
- **stdin** — standard input (default: keyboard)
- **stdout** — standard output (default: screen)
- **stderr** — error output (separate from normal output)

Python's `print()` writes to stdout. `input()` reads from stdin. AI training scripts use these constantly for progress logging.

### I/O is Usually the Bottleneck

In ML training:
- GPU compute: milliseconds per batch
- Loading images from disk: also milliseconds — often SLOWER than compute

Data loading is I/O. A slow data pipeline means your expensive GPU sits idle waiting for the next batch. PyTorch's DataLoader uses multiple worker processes specifically to parallelise I/O so the GPU never waits.
common_mistakes: Assuming compute is always the bottleneck — often it's disk I/O (reading training data) or network I/O (downloading data)
Not separating stderr from stdout in training logs — errors get mixed with progress output and are hard to find
Loading entire datasets into RAM when they're too large — use streaming/lazy I/O instead
mini_exercise: In Python, try: x = input('Enter your name: '). Then print('Hello, ' + x). You've just used stdin and stdout directly. Now think: in an AI system, what would replace input() and print()?
